artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Kids are finally down, so I’m catching my breath and trying to make sense of the last year or so. It’s weird to think how different things feel now compared to that stretch from March–May 2024 when Karen Li slapped me with the formal PIP after the missed patient portal deployment at the Damen campus. I still remember sitting in Conference Room B on March 11, 2024 with her and Mark Donovan from HR, heart pounding, doing the math on how we’d cover the $1,750 rent on Belmont if I blew it. Those 60–65 hour weeks wrecked my sleep and my back.

The crazy part is how much has shifted since then. My back pain really has gone from a constant 7/10 to more like a 3–4/10 most days. I can get through a full 8–9 hour workday now without lying on the office floor like I did twice in November 2024 when the Epic rollout panic attacks were at their worst. I even sat through the full 3‑hour steering committee on January 9, 2026 in the 9th‑floor conference room without the sciatica flare that had me leaning on the wall during the April 11, 2025 go‑live review with Sandra.

I keep thinking about how close I felt to losing everything back in May 2023 when I was on the Social Security site at 1 a.m. running SSDI calculators, convinced I’d end up like Dad’s cousin Miguel. Between the chiro, the walking loops at Horner, and actually sleeping more than 4 hours a night, I finally feel like I’m not on that path.

Anyway, tomorrow’s another 7:00 a.m. check‑in downtown with Karen Liu for this new PIP, so I should log off. Thanks for sticking with me through all of this.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Catching up on the last couple years

artifacts.content:
Personal health + work check-in – 2026-01-22

Back/physical:
- Day-to-day pain really is closer to 3–4/10 now instead of that constant 7/10 I was living with last year. I can sit 60–90 minutes at the desk without feeling like my spine is going to snap, and I’m not rolling out of bed like an old man every morning.
- Huge milestone: made it through the full 3‑hour steering committee on 1/9/26 (9:00–12:00 in the 9th‑floor room at Midwest Health Systems HQ) without the right‑leg tingling I had during the April 11, 2025 Epic upgrade review.
- House stuff: carried two full laundry baskets (20–25 lbs each) plus the Costco detergent up from the basement last weekend, no stabbing pain like after Daniel’s Kedzie eviction move in March 2024 when I had to cancel a workday.
- Still need to respect limits after that dumb weighted‑squat twinge three weeks ago.

Energy/stress:
- Panic attacks way down compared to late October 2024 when I started regular CBT with Dr. Patel at Northwestern Behavioral Health in Lakeview; was having 2–3 a week, now maybe one mild one every 10–14 days. Haven’t touched the PRN clonazepam since early December 2024.
- Blood pressure trending better since early August 2024: 128/82 at Dr. Karen Liu’s employee clinic visit on 11/4/24 vs 142/90 in May. Still need to keep up Horner Park walks and less salty team lunches.
- Work stress spiking again with Karen Liu’s new PIP after the Epic milestone miss in early Dec 2025 (7 a.m. downtown check‑ins, +10–12 hrs/week). Neck/upper‑back tightest Sunday nights.

Family:
- Dad’s early‑stage CHF at Northwestern (dx mid‑Oct 2025) + twice‑monthly cardiology runs = more car time, more back stiffness. Use lumbar cushion every trip.
- Still want enough stamina by September 2025 to handle the 0.8‑mile school walks twice a day if Emily can’t drive.

Subject: Quick update on Dad, the kids, and my back

Hi Mami,

Just wanted to send you an update since things have been busy.

First, about Dad: I know the early‑stage congestive heart failure diagnosis at Northwestern Memorial back in mid‑October 2025 scared all of us. I’ve been taking him to cardiology follow‑ups about twice a month. With traffic and waiting rooms it’s 3–4 hours each visit, but the doctors on the 4th‑floor clinic have been patient. I’m using that lumbar cushion in the car so my lower back doesn’t lock up like it used to after long drives.

My own health is better than when I saw Dr. Karen Liu at the Midwest employee clinic on November 4, 2024. That day my blood pressure was 128/82 instead of the 142/90 she got back in May 2024, so she let me hold off on lisinopril as long as I kept walking around Horner Park and cut back on salty lunches. I’m still doing that, and the chiropractor’s exercises have helped my posture. My daily back pain is more like a 3/10 now, so I can get through work without lying on the office floor like I did twice in November 2024 during the Epic rollout panic.

The kids are okay. Lucas is doing better at Audubon since his IEP got finalized in mid‑November 2023 after that October 3 meeting about his ADHD and the playground pushing. Sofia is still her silly self and loves our Tuesday family swims at McFetridge. By mid‑December 2025 I was finally able to stay in the pool the whole 45 minutes with them without that scary chest tightness I had back in April 2025 when Dr. Mehta first mentioned hypertension.

We’ll try to come by Little Village on Sunday after mass.

Te quiero,
Michael

metadata.subject: Quick update on Dad, the kids, and my back

artifacts.content:
Daniel: You alive or buried under Epic tickets?

Michael: Somewhere in between. Karen Liu put me on another PIP in early Dec after we slipped a deployment milestone, so I’ve got 7 a.m. check‑ins downtown every Monday and like 10–12 extra hours a week. Shoulders feel like concrete again.

Daniel: Dude. That sucks. How’s your back holding up?

Michael: Weirdly, better than last year. Daily pain’s more like a 3–4 now instead of that 7/10 where I kept lying on my office floor during those November 2024 panic attacks about the Epic rollout. Chiro + stretches help, and I’m not getting those 9/10 spikes from just sitting.

Daniel: At least something’s improving.

Michael: Yeah. Remember when your Kedzie eviction in March 2024 basically wrecked me for a week after we hauled boxes for 6 hours? Now I can do our whole laundry run in 40 min, two full baskets (20–25 lbs) up to 3B, and still have enough in the tank to kick a ball with Lucas in the alley.

Daniel: Speaking of wrecked, my court stuff still stressing you out?

Michael: Less than your DUI did. That week of your hearing at Cook County last April when we stayed up till 2 a.m. at Mom and Dad’s going over the $6,800 lawyer bill—my energy was shot and my neck locked up every time your number popped up. Compared to that and my own near‑DUI in Logan Square (0.11 on the breathalyzer on 9/7/24—thanks again to Feldman), this work PIP is annoying but not end‑of‑the‑world.

Daniel: Lol yeah, let’s not repeat the Logan Square special.

Michael: For real. I’m down to 1–2 drinks on weekends now. Sleep’s better, fewer panic attacks. Still tense, but not totally wrecked.

Event: Tuesday Family Swim – McFetridge Sports Center
Date: 2026-01-26
Time: 6:15–7:15 p.m.
Location: McFetridge Sports Center pool
Attendees: Michael, Emily, Lucas, Sofia

Notes:
- Leave Belmont by 5:35 p.m. to find parking and get kids changed.
- Bring: goggles for Lucas (blue ones), Sofia’s float belt, my own trunks, small towel for my lower back when we sit on the bleachers.
- Health check-in: Since mid‑December 2025 I’ve actually been able to stay in the pool the full 45‑minute open swim without the crushing fatigue and chest tightness I had back in April 2025 when Dr. Priya Mehta at the Midwest internal medicine clinic started talking about borderline hypertension and bumped the lisinopril to 10 mg. Keep an eye on how my back and shoulders feel after helping Lucas with freestyle drills.
- BP: Bring the $60 portable cuff in the car and take a reading afterward; last good post‑swim number was 128/82. Goal is to keep it in that range so I don’t end up where I was last July at Advocate Illinois Masonic with that 165/105 scare after the Lakefront Trail walk.
- Energy: Notice if I still have enough in the tank after swim to handle the chaotic 5–8 p.m. window (dishes, baths, bedtime stories) without a tension headache.
- Back: Do 10 minutes of core work at home before we leave—dead bugs + modified plank—to avoid the lower‑back seizing I used to get after just 15 minutes in the shallow end.

Subject: Checking in (and asking a favor)

Hey Coach,

Hope you’re doing okay and that the Horner Park fields aren’t total mud pits right now.

I’ve been thinking a lot about this coming season with Lucas’s U10 team. You probably remember last April when I tweaked my back at his practice and ended up at Swedish Hospital ER for imaging after barely 10 minutes of drills. That was kind of my wake‑up call. Since then I’ve been doing a lot of work on my back—chiro, core exercises, walking loops at Horner—and my daily pain is down from a 7/10 to more like a 3–4/10. I can actually bend to tie my shoes without bracing myself.

My goal is that by Spring 2025 I can play a full 45–60 minutes of soccer with the kids without worrying I’ll repeat the Swedish ER fiasco. I’d also really like to be able to commit to the full 3‑hour Saturday practices by November 2025, instead of bailing like I did three times this spring when the overnight on‑call shifts for the Epic rollout at Midwest Health Systems triggered brutal migraine flares.

Work is still nuts (I’m on another performance plan after a missed Epic milestone in early December 2025—fun times), but my energy is more stable than it was during that March–May 2024 PIP when I was working 60–65 hours a week and waking up at 3 a.m. replaying the HR meeting in Conference Room B.

Favor: when the season schedule is set, can you send me the practice times as early as possible? I want to block off my calendar so Karen can’t drop late‑night deployments on those Saturday mornings.

Thanks again for being so good with Lucas.

Michael

Title: Money + driving stress – action list
Date: 2026-01-22

1. HOA dues – 2847 W Belmont
- We’re two months behind on the condo association dues from back in September 2025.
- Current balance per the letter: $1,050.
- They warned about “possible legal action” and even mentioned a lien if we don’t catch up by end of January.
- No wonder I’m lying awake with mid‑back tightness, then dragging through 9 a.m. stand‑ups.
- To‑do: call HOA office tomorrow, ask about a payment plan so we don’t get hit all at once.

2. IL Secretary of State ticket
- Early January 2026 notice: risk of license suspension because I ignored that $250 Kennedy Expressway speed camera ticket from July 2025 (plus late fees).
- If I lose my license, I can’t get Dad to Northwestern for his CHF cardiology follow‑ups twice a month or make the 7 a.m. PIP check‑ins downtown with Karen Liu.
- To‑do: pay online this weekend, set calendar reminder so I don’t forget.

3. Work stress + health
- Still on the formal PIP since early Dec 2025 after we slipped the Epic upgrade milestone. 10–12 extra hours/week, Monday 7 a.m. meetings.
- Notice: neck and upper‑back tension spikes Sunday nights; tension headaches creeping back toward 2x/week instead of 1x.
- Plan: keep movement breaks every 60–90 minutes, no skipping evening core work more than one day in a row, even if I’m on late calls with the India team.

4. Sanity
- Remember: not where I was in late October 2024 (2–3 panic attacks/week, PRN clonazepam) or May 2023 (SSDI calculator spiral). Things are stressful, not hopeless.
